aliases: Caramel Char Siew Rice 蜜汁叉烧饭

english: Caramel Char Siew Rice 蜜汁叉烧饭

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Caramel Char Siew Rice; mapped to the broader 'rice dish' class (FOODON_00002678) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0FJP (rice-based composite dish) used as the closest defensible category; no specific code exists for Singaporean char siew rice preparations.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific dish; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Caramel Char Siew Rice as served at a Singaporean hawker centre or coffee shop typically weighs approximately 350 g, comprising a bowl of steamed white rice topped with several slices of caramel-glazed char siew and a ladle of sweet-savoury gravy.

nutrient_source.title: Caramel Char Siew Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Caramel Char Siew Rice (蜜汁叉烧饭), cr_id G00010703, with confidence 0.95. All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is not reported in the HPB entry and is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for BBQ pork (char siu) and cooked white rice composite; see notes.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values 2021 — white rice (boiled, Asian-style) and composite rice dish analogues
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Caramel Char Siew Rice. GI of 72 is assigned based on the dominant carbohydrate component: boiled white rice (Asian-style, GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database). The caramel glaze and gravy contribute additional rapidly-digested sugars, supporting a high-GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (72 × 88.2 g carbohydrate in 350 g serving) / 100 ≈ 63.5. Available carbohydrate per 350 g serving = 25.19 × 3.5 = 88.2 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 231 mg/100 g from soy sauce, hoisin, and cured pork; partially offset by potassium 327 mg/100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced gravy and minimal caramel glaze, as the soy-based sauces and cured pork contribute significant sodium; the favourable sodium-to-potassium ratio (0.71) provides partial mitigation, but a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 809 mg sodium — about one-third of the 2 300 mg daily limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycaemic index (~72) from white rice combined with caramel and honey glaze sugars; 25.19 g/100 g carbohydrate yields a glycaemic load of ~63.5 per standard serving
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high glycaemic load; substituting white rice with brown rice or cauliflower rice, requesting minimal caramel glaze, and adding non-starchy vegetables can substantially reduce the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork char siew (~1.4 g/100 g estimated), sodium 231 mg/100 g, and high-temperature caramelisation generating advanced glycation end-products (AGEs)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size and frequency of consumption, remove visible fatty portions of char siew, and request reduced gravy to lower saturated fat and sodium intake; the high-temperature caramelisation process generates AGEs that may contribute to endothelial dysfunction and arterial stiffness.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork (red meat) is rich in arachidonic acid (omega-6) which amplifies inflammatory cytokines; high-temperature caramelisation generates AGEs that worsen joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise consumption of this dish; substituting char siew with chicken or tofu, requesting minimal caramel glaze, and reducing frequency of high-temperature roasted meats can help lower pro-inflammatory AGE and arachidonic acid load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Caramel Char Siew Rice (蜜汁叉烧饭) is a popular Singaporean Chinese hawker dish consisting of steamed white rice topped with slices of caramel-glazed char siew (BBQ roast pork) and finished with a sweet-savoury gravy. Char siew is made by marinating pork shoulder or belly in a mixture of honey, maltose, hoisin sauce, soy sauce, five-spice powder, and red fermented tofu, then roasting at high heat to achieve a lacquered, caramelised exterior with a tender, slightly fatty interior. The caramel glaze imparts a distinctive amber colour and a sweet-smoky flavour profile. Per 100 g, the dish provides 164 kcal, 6.5 g protein, 4.21 g fat, and 25.19 g carbohydrate, with notable dietary fibre (3.7 g), potassium (327 mg), calcium (54 mg), and iron (2.55 mg) from the HPB Singapore Foodo database. The dish carries a high glycaemic index (~72) driven by white rice and the caramel-honey glaze, making it unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes without modification. Sodium at 231 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management, though the potassium content provides partial offset. The high-temperature roasting process generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs), which are of concern for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis and cardiovascular disease. Commonly served at hawker centres, coffee shops (kopitiams), and Chinese BBQ stalls across Singapore, this dish is a staple lunch and dinner option in the local food culture.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010703, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy composite for BBQ pork and white rice; this single estimated field does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the strong primary source coverage of all other fields.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.4 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for BBQ pork (char siu, USDA FDC ID 174032) and cooked white rice composite, as the HPB record does not report saturated fat separately. This is the only field not directly sourced from HPB. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, vitamin micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this specific Singaporean caramel char siew rice preparation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. cholesterol_mg is reported as 0.0 in the HPB source record; this is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (cr_id G00010703) — this value is unusual for a pork-containing dish and may reflect a reporting limitation of the HPB composite entry; consumers should be aware that char siew pork typically contains dietary cholesterol. 4. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on boiled white rice (Asian-style) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for caramel char siew rice was identified in approved sources. 5. The dish is not halal-certified as it contains pork (char siew). 6. Ontology mapped to broader FoodOn rice dish class and FoodEx2 A0FJP as no specific codes exist for this dish.
